title: Graphs
description: Graph representation with adjacency lists using arrays, DFS, BFS, and connected components — core patterns for graph interview problems in C.

sections.title: Adjacency list with arrays
sections.explanation: In C, represent a graph's adjacency list using 2D arrays or parallel arrays. A simple approach: `int adj[MAX_N][MAX_N]` for the neighbor list and `int deg[MAX_N]` for the degree (number of neighbors) of each node. Add an edge by appending to both nodes' lists for undirected graphs.

exercise.prompt: Build an undirected graph with 4 nodes and edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 2-3. Print the neighbors of each node in format "node: n1 n2 ...".

validation.message: Each node should list its neighbors
Use adjacency list with degree tracking for undirected graph

exercise.hint: add_edge adds v to u's list and u to v's list. deg tracks how many neighbors each node has.

sections.title: DFS on graph
sections.explanation: DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking. Use a **visited** array to avoid cycles. Start from a node, mark it visited, then recursively visit all unvisited neighbors. DFS is the basis for cycle detection, topological sort, and path finding.

exercise.prompt: Perform DFS starting from node 0 on graph with edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 2-3. Print visited nodes in DFS order.

validation.message: DFS from 0 visits: 0, then 1 (first neighbor), then 3, then 2
Mark visited before recursing, check !visited before visiting neighbors

exercise.hint: DFS from 0: visit 0, go to neighbor 1, from 1 go to 3, backtrack, visit 2. Order: 0 1 3 2.

sections.title: BFS on graph
sections.explanation: BFS explores level by level using a queue. It finds the **shortest path** in unweighted graphs. Use an array queue of node indices with front/back pointers. Mark nodes visited when **enqueued** (not when dequeued) to avoid duplicates.

exercise.prompt: Perform BFS starting from node 0 on graph with edges: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 2-3. Print visited nodes in BFS order.

validation.message: BFS from 0 visits level by level: 0, then 1 2, then 3
Use array queue, mark visited on enqueue

exercise.hint: BFS visits level by level: 0 first, then neighbors 1 and 2, then 3. Mark visited when enqueuing.

sections.title: Connected components
sections.explanation: To find connected components, iterate through all nodes. For each unvisited node, run DFS/BFS to mark all reachable nodes as one component. Count how many times you start a new DFS — that's the number of components.

exercise.prompt: Find the number of connected components in a graph with 6 nodes and edges: 0-1, 1-2, 3-4. Nodes 5 is isolated. Print the count.

validation.message: Loop through all nodes, DFS from unvisited ones, increment component count

exercise.hint: Start DFS from each unvisited node. Components: {0,1,2}, {3,4}, {5} = 3 components.
